PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * **New Year's Festival** (January 1) * **First Shrine Visit** (January 1–31) * **Kinen-sai** (February 17) * **Reitaisai** (April 28) * **War Memorial Peace Festival** (August 15) * **Shichi-Go-San Festival** (November 15) * **Niiname-sai** (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]]{{Gokoku shrines}}[[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Kk]] [[ja:栃木県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * **New Year's Festival** (January 1) * **First Shrine Visit** (January 1–31) * **Kinen-sai** (February 17) * **Reitaisai** (April 28) * **War Memorial Peace Festival** (August 15) * **Shichi-Go-San Festival** (November 15) * **Niiname-sai** (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi revision.text:
{{draft}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{Location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * **New Year's Festival** (January 1) * **First Shrine Visit** (January 1–31) * **Kinen-sai** (February 17) * **Reitaisai** (April 28) * **War Memorial Peace Festival** (August 15) * **Shichi-Go-San Festival** (November 15) * **Niiname-sai** (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Gokoku shrines]] good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture {{draft}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{Location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages that were the (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == {{draft}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]]{{Gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[ja:栃木県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{Shinto-stub}}

news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku {{draft}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{Gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:Unsorted link categories]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[ja:栃木県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} revision.text:
{{draft}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{Gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:栃木県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{Shinto-stub}}

It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{Gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{draft}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{Gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{draft}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{Gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:栃木県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{Shinto-stub}}
