They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]]{{Gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[ja:栃木県護国神社]] }} {{Shinto-stub}} \[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Qqqq]] [[Category:New]] {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 3]]

Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall.== See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65年 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]]{{Gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[ja:栃木県護国神社]] }} {{Shinto-stub}} \[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:New]] {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65th year 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65th year 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65th year 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Categories]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65th year 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]]{{Gokoku shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Tochigi Gokoku everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[ja:栃木県護国神社]] }} {{Shinto-stub}} \[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:New]] {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tochigi It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65th year 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市|12=draft}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社|12=draft}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|12=draft}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市|12=draft}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕|12=draft}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65th year 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹|12=draft}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} }} {{Shinto-stub}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:ochigi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:栃木県護国神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}

View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市|12=draft}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社|1=Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|12=draft}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the [[Imperial Household Agency]] inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|1=Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|12=draft}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya, Tochigi|ja|宇都宮市|12=draft}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the [[Boshin War]], [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[Pacific War]] as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the [[Japan Self-Defense Forces]]. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕|12=draft}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a [[Home Ministry]]-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in [[kamikaze]] missions during the [[Pacific War]].{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65th year 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a [[:en:Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|Mitsubishi A6M Zero]] fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹|12=draft}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} }} {{Shinto-stub}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Utsunomiya]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meiji period establishments]] [[Category:Pacific War memorials]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:draft:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Tochigi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:ochigi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:栃木県護国神社]] {{citation needed|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}
