revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Tochigiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:栃木県護国神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tochigiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:栃木県護国神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tochigiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:栃木県護国神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya, Tochigi|1=Utsunomiya}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社|1=Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|1=Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya, Tochigi|1=Utsunomiya}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}}, {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}}, {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, and {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the {{ill|Japan Self-Defense Forces|en|Japan Self-Defense Forces|WD=Q275488|lt=Japan Self-Defense Forces|1=Japan Self-Defense Forces}}. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Toda Tadayuki|lt=Toda Tadayuki|lt=Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadaaki Toda|qq=}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Home Ministry|1=Home Ministry}}-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in {{ill|Kamikaze|en|Kamikaze|WD=Q133194|lt=Kamikaze|1=Kamikaze}} missions during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}.{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65th year 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a {{ill|Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|en|Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|WD=Q40156|lt=Mitsubishi A6M Zero|1=Mitsubishi A6M Zero}} fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toru Funamura|qq=}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.gokoku.gr.jp Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] - Official Website * [http://www.tochigi-jinjacho.or.jp/?p=594 Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine - Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Agency] * {{Twitter|tochigi_gokoku}} * {{Facebook|137284933107090}} }} {{Shinto-stub}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|栃木県護国神社|version=99332893|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} {{everybodywiki link|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:栃木県護国神社]] {{Commons category|Tochigiken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11535309}} [[Category:100 Views of Utsunomiya]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Utsunomiya]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[co:commons:Category:Tochigiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:栃木県護国神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tochigiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:栃木県護国神社]] [[ja:栃木県護国神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Tochigi Gokoku-jinja 001.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya, Tochigi|1=Utsunomiya}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan | latitude = 36.57222 | longitude = 139.85972 | deities = War dead, including 55,361 spirits from Tochigi Prefecture | founded = 1872 (Meiji 5) | structure = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = April 28 | map = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=220|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-latitude=36.565|frame-longitude=139.872}} |Wikimedia maps display |{{location map|Japan Tochigi|float=center|width=220|label={{nowrap|Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}|position=left}} |Display Tochigi Prefecture map }} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社|1=Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} In 1996 before a visit the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} inquired about potential war criminals at the shrine. {{Citation |title=Yasukuni Fundamentalism |date=2021-07-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1jpf1pk.11 |work= |pages=207 |access-date=2023-10-25 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press}} '''Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|栃木県護国神社|1=Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|Tochigi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}) located in {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya, Tochigi|1=Utsunomiya}}, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan. It honors spirits from Tochigi Prefecture who died in wars such as the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}}, {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}}, {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, and {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} as well as deceased police officers, firefighters, and members of the {{ill|Japan Self-Defense Forces|en|Japan Self-Defense Forces|WD=Q275488|lt=Japan Self-Defense Forces|1=Japan Self-Defense Forces}}. == History == The shrine traces its origins to 1872 (Meiji 5) when 97 spirits from the Boshin War, including those of Lord {{ill|Toda Tadayuki|lt=Toda Tadayuki|lt=Tadaaki Toda|ja|戸田忠恕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadaaki Toda|qq=}}, the former [[daimyo]] of Utsunomiya, and his retainers, were enshrined in Utsunomiya's Baba Town at the site of the original {{nihongo|Utsunomiya Shōkonsha|宇都宮招魂社}}.栃木県神社誌, p. 221 In 1939 (Showa 14), the shrine was designated a {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Home Ministry|1=Home Ministry}}-approved Gokoku Shrine and renamed Tochigi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. It was relocated to its current site in 1940 (Showa 15). After the war, it was briefly renamed {{nihongo|Shōtoku Shrine|彰徳神社}} in 1947, but reverted to its former name in 1953 following the withdrawal of occupation forces. On July 25, 1996 (Heisei 8), Emperor {{nihongo|Akihito|上皇明仁}} and Empress {{nihongo|Michiko|上皇后美智子}} officially visited and paid their respects at the shrine. The event marked the first instance of a Gokoku Shrine being officially visited by the Emperor and Empress. In 2010 (Heisei 22), a prominent Japanese evergreen oak tree within the shrine grounds was transplanted to make way for road expansion, demonstrating efforts to preserve the area's heritage.{{cite news|title=見守られて引っ越し 護国神社のシイの木 地域のシンボルへ期待|newspaper=[[下野新聞]]|date=2010-03-19}} The shrine also hosts memorials, including a monument dedicated in 2010 to young men who perished in {{ill|Kamikaze|en|Kamikaze|WD=Q133194|lt=Kamikaze|1=Kamikaze}} missions during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}.{{cite news|title=とちぎ戦後65th year 節目に「[[特攻勇士の像]]」 元将校ら「語り継ぎたい」 宇都宮の護国神社 あす除幕式|newspaper=下野新聞|date=2010-08-14}} == Annual Events == * '''New Year's Festival''' (January 1) * '''First Shrine Visit''' (January 1–31) * '''Kinen-sai''' (February 17) * '''Reitaisai''' (April 28) * '''War Memorial Peace Festival''' (August 15) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Niiname-sai''' (November 23) == Notable Features == The shrine grounds include a model of a {{ill|Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|en|Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero|WD=Q40156|lt=Mitsubishi A6M Zero|1=Mitsubishi A6M Zero}} fighter plane, donated by famed composer {{ill|Toru Funamura|ja|船村徹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toru Funamura|qq=}}, displayed near the main hall. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} ==
